narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and a family from a high-income bracket, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plans to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 59% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help ensure we admit students most likely to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by two key factors that pushed the model toward expecting a bar failure: your LSAT score of 22.50, which is notably below the typical applicant's 36.70 and had the strongest negative influence, and your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far lower than the average of 5.53, which was the second-biggest concern. On a more positive note, your high family income and above-average undergrad GPA of 3.30 compared to the 3.21 norm worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage success. Your top decile in Year 1 and full-time enrollment plans offered smaller boosts but couldn't outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical students in our dataset, who often have much higher LSATs and mid-range decile rankings around the 5th or 6th, your profile stands out in those weaker areas, tipping the scales despite your other strengths. I know this rejection stings, especially with your promising GPA and background, but it's not a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the average, as it carries the most weight here, and reflect on what might have impacted your later-year performance to strengthen future applications. You might also seek targeted prep courses or mentorship tailored for bar success. Many students in similar situations rebound stronger—keep pursuing your goals, and we wish you the best in your next steps.
